title: SEGMENT 2 — The pre-history: Solexa, 1998 Cambridge, Balasubramanian and Klenerman

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The pre-history: Solexa, 1998 Cambridge, Balasubramanian and Klenerman
Illumina sequencing did not begin at Illumina.
It began at the University of Cambridge in the late 1990s, in the chemistry department, in the lab of two young researchers named Shankar Balasubramanian and David Klenerman.

The story of how their academic curiosity became, two decades later, the dominant sequencing technology on Earth is worth telling, because it's a beautiful case study in how a piece of basic-science work — driven by an interest that had nothing to do with sequencing at first — becomes a globally important technology.
In 1997 and 1998, Balasubramanian and Klenerman were not trying to build a sequencer.
They were trying to understand how DNA polymerase — the enzyme that copies DNA — works at the level of single molecules.
The fundamental question was a physical one: can we watch a polymerase add a single nucleotide to a growing DNA strand and detect that single event optically?

Both of them were chemists with strong interests in fluorescence, and their idea was to attach a fluorescent dye to a single nucleotide, drop a single DNA molecule with a polymerase onto a glass surface, and watch the polymerase pull in fluorescent nucleotides one at a time.
This was a basic-science exercise.
The goal was understanding the enzyme.
But here is the moment of realization.
They sat down in a pub — there is, depending on who tells the story, a specific Cambridge pub involved — and they started thinking about what their setup actually amounted to.
If you could detect single nucleotides being incorporated one at a time, by a polymerase copying a template, then in principle you were sequencing that template.
Watch which fluorescent color flashes each cycle, and you've just read out a letter.
Repeat over and over, and you read out a sequence.
Their basic-science fluorescence experiment was, structurally, a primitive sequencing reaction.
The leap from "this is a single-molecule biophysics tool" to "this is a sequencing platform" took place over the course of one conversation.
The technical idea that made the whole thing work was something called reversible terminator chemistry.
Here's the problem you have to solve.

If you just add four fluorescent nucleotides — fluorescent A, fluorescent T, fluorescent G, fluorescent C — to a polymerase and a template, the polymerase will add them as fast as it can, and you'll get a long synthesized strand with no way to read out one base at a time.
The polymerase will add ten or twenty bases before you can take a photograph and figure out what happened.
You need a way to make the polymerase add exactly one nucleotide, then stop, then wait while you take a picture, then resume.
You need a brake.

The brake they invented — building on earlier chemistry from Bruno Canard and Simon Sarfati at the Pasteur Institute in Paris — was to chemically modify each nucleotide so that, after it gets incorporated into the growing strand, it physically blocks the polymerase from adding the next one.
The blocker sits on the three-prime hydroxyl group of the nucleotide — the very chemical handle the polymerase needs to grab in order to attach the next base.
Block that handle and the polymerase stalls, frozen at exactly one new base added.
Take a photograph to see which dye lit up.
Then chemically remove the blocker, washing it off with a mild reagent, freeing up the handle again.
Now the polymerase can add the next nucleotide.
Add the next round of fluorescent blocked nucleotides, repeat.
One base per cycle.
Take a picture.
Cleave.
Repeat.

The elegance of the chemistry is that the same modification that carries the dye also carries the blocker, so when you chemically remove the blocker you also remove the dye, leaving a clean, unmodified nucleotide ready to extend further.
One molecule, two functions, both reversed in one step.
Balasubramanian and Klenerman founded a company in 1998 called Solexa — the name a play on "solution exotic" or simply a catchy mash-up; the etymology is a bit murky — to commercialize this idea.

For about eight years, Solexa was a small British startup, mostly invisible to the outside world, slowly working through every engineering problem you have to solve to turn a clever chemistry idea into a robust instrument.
There were a lot of those problems.
How do you immobilize DNA on a surface densely enough to be useful but sparsely enough that you can see individual signals?
How do you amplify a single DNA molecule's signal so a camera can detect it?
How do you make the polymerase tolerate the bulky modifications without slowing to a crawl?
How do you do hundreds of wash steps in a row without the chemistry degrading?
Every one of these is a separate research project, and Solexa solved them one by one over the better part of a decade.
In 2007, Illumina, an American company that had built its reputation on a different platform — bead arrays for genotyping — bought Solexa for about six hundred and fifty million dollars.
At the time, this was a striking acquisition; Illumina was betting that next-generation sequencing was about to take off, and Solexa's reversible terminator platform was the most promising candidate.
The bet paid off spectacularly.

Within five years, Illumina sequencing had effectively become the standard, displacing the competing technologies of the time — pyrosequencing from a company called 454, ligation-based sequencing from a platform called SOLiD, and a few others — into commercial obscurity.
By 2014, the HiSeq X Ten launch announcement claimed the long-promised "thousand-dollar genome," and the dominance was complete.
What I want you to take from this segment is the lineage.
The technology is sometimes called Illumina sequencing, sometimes Solexa sequencing, sometimes sequencing by synthesis, sometimes SBS, sometimes reversible terminator sequencing.
These all refer to the same thing — the chemistry invented by Balasubramanian and Klenerman in Cambridge in the late 1990s, commercialized by Solexa, scaled up by Illumina.
The fundamental chemistry has not changed in twenty-five years.
What changed is the engineering around it — the density of clusters, the speed of imaging, the throughput of the optics, the chemistry's tolerance to longer reads.
The cost reductions of the last two decades come almost entirely from engineering improvements layered on top of the same core invention.
That's worth remembering: a single chemical insight, refined and scaled relentlessly, can collapse the cost of an entire field by seven orders of magnitude.
Now let's actually open the hood and look at the parts.
